6: exousian echei ho huios tou anthropou epi tes ges aphienai hamartias — the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — the two claims in sequence: (1) the Son of Man (Danielic title) exercises divine prerogatives, (2) epi tes ges (on earth) — this authority is exercised in history, not deferred to final judgment. The scribes' objection is theologically correct (only God can forgive sins); Jesus's claim is equally precise: the Son of Man is acting with God's authority on earth.
13: eleos thelo kai ou thysian — mercy I desire and not sacrifice — the Hosea citation (Hos 6:6); eleos (steadfast covenant-love, translating Hebrew hesed) is the Covenant-quality Jesus consistently prioritizes over external cultic performance. ouk elthon kalesai dikaious alla hamartoulous — I did not come to call righteous people but sinners — kaleo (to call) carries the election-language of divine summoning; the call of sinners is the unexpected reversal that defines Jesus's mission-profile.
1: embas eis ploion diepersen — stepping into a boat he crossed over — embas aorist participle of embainō; diepersen aorist of diaperaō (to cross over completely): a purposeful journey back. Eis tēn idian polin — to his own town: Capernaum (cf. 4:13), which Matthew treats as Jesus's base of operations. The return frames the healing of the paralytic that follows as a homecoming demonstration.
2: idōn tēn pistin autōn — seeing their faith — idōn aorist participle of horaō: Jesus perceives the faith not just of the paralytic but of the plural autōn (them), the group who brought him. Tharsei teknon — take heart, child (teknon, child: an affectionate address expressing personal relationship). Aphientai sou hai hamartiai — your sins are being forgiven (aphientai present passive: the forgiving is happening now, not merely declared complete). The present tense is significant: not "were forgiven" but "are forgiven in this moment."
3: houtoi blasphēmei — this man is blaspheming — the scribes' accusation is theologically precise: forgiveness of sins was recognized as a divine prerogative (cf. Isa 43:25; Ps 103:3); claiming to exercise it is, on their framework, either divine authority or blasphemy. The scribes do not say Jesus is wrong about the man's forgiveness; they say he has no right to announce it. En heautois — within themselves: the accusation is unspoken, a thought Jesus perceives (v.4).
4: gnous de ho Iēsous tas enthymēseis autōn — knowing their deliberations/thoughts (enthymēseis, from enthymeomai: deep inner deliberation, the reasoning process of the heart, not surface thought) — gnous aorist participle of ginōskō: Jesus's knowledge of their internal reasoning is direct, not inferred from facial expressions. Eis tas kardias hymōn — in your hearts: the heart (kardia) as the seat of deliberation reinforces that the scribes' error is not intellectual but volitional.
5: ti gar estin eukopōteron — for which is easier (eukopoteros, comparative of eukopos: easy, requiring little labor) — the question is rhetorical and exposes the asymmetry: saying "your sins are forgiven" is unverifiable (no physical change occurs); saying "get up and walk" is immediately verifiable. If Jesus can do the harder (visible) thing, it demonstrates the authority behind the easier-to-say (invisible) thing. The miracles are epistemological evidence.
7: kai ēgerthē — and he arose (egeirō aorist passive: to be raised up, the same verb used for resurrection; the healing terminology anticipates resurrection-language throughout Matthew) — apēlthen eis ton oikon autou (went to his house): the brevity of the report is striking; Matthew does not narrate the mechanics of the healing but the completed result. The aorist marks the action as complete and decisive.
8: idontes de hoi ochloi ephobēthēsan — seeing this, the crowds were afraid (phobeō aorist passive: a sudden awe-filled fear) — edoxasan ton theon (glorified God): the fear produces doxology, not paralysis. Ton donta exousian toiautēn tois anthrōpois — the one who gave such authority to human beings: the crowd's conclusion is partial; they see "authority given to human beings" but do not yet perceive the divine identity of the one exercising it. The theology of the crowds remains incomplete.
9: Maththaion legomenon kathēmenon epi to telōnion — a man named Matthew sitting at the tax collector's booth — telōnion (the toll-collection booth, the official station for collecting customs dues); kathēmenon present participle: he was seated, engaged in his work, when the call came. Akolouthei moi — follow me (present imperative: begin following and continue). Anastas ēkolouthēsen autō — rising he followed him — anastas aorist participle (having risen): the immediacy of response echoes 4:20,22 (the fishermen disciples).
10: anakeiomnou autou en tē oikia — while he was reclining at table in the house (anakeimai, to recline at a meal, the formal dining posture) — polloi telōnai kai hamartōloi (many tax collectors and sinners): telōnai (toll-collectors, collaborators with Herod/Rome; despised both as extortioners and as ritually unclean from constant Gentile contact) and hamartōloi (sinners, those either openly transgressing Torah or in professions that made observance impossible). Synanekeninto — were reclining together: shared table-fellowship implied social and covenantal solidarity.
11: dia ti meta tōn telōnōn kai hamartōlōn esthiei ho didaskalos hymōn — why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners — the question is addressed to the disciples, not Jesus; the Pharisees use the social pressure of questioning subordinates rather than confronting the teacher directly. Didaskalos (teacher): the Pharisees acknowledge Jesus's role while challenging his practice. Table fellowship in Second Temple Judaism marked social and religious boundaries; eating with the unclean signaled solidarity with them.
12: ou chreian echousin hoi ischyontes iatrou — the strong do not have need of a physician — ischyontes present participle of ischyō (to be strong, to be well): those who are currently strong. The analogy reframes the question: a physician eating with the sick is not contaminated but is practicing medicine. Hoi kakōs echontes — those who are ill (having it badly): the idiom for illness sets up the mission-statement of v.13. The implication: the Pharisees misidentify who needs the physician and therefore misread the physician's table practice.
14: dia ti hēmeis kai hoi Pharisaioi nēsteuomen polla — why do we and the Pharisees fast often (nēsteuō, present: ongoing fasting practice; polla = much, often) — John's disciples and the Pharisees share a fasting practice despite their theological differences, presenting a united front of puzzlement. Hoi de mathētai sou ou nēsteuousin — but your disciples do not fast: the contrast is between communities defined by their devotional disciplines. Jesus's response offers a theology of the eschatological moment that explains the discrepancy.
15: mē dynasthai penthein hoi huioi tou nymphōnos — can the sons of the bridal chamber mourn (pentheō as in the second Beatitude; now applied to the disciples' non-fasting) — hoi huioi tou nymphōnos (the sons of the bridal chamber, the wedding guests who attend the groom): a Semitic idiom for those intimately associated with the bridegroom. Heōs hotou met autōn estin ho numphios — as long as the bridegroom is with them. Eleusontai de hēmerai hotan aparthē ap autōn ho numphios, kai tote nēsteusousin — but days will come when the bridegroom is taken from them (aparthē aorist passive subjunctive of apairō: taken away, the language of violent removal), and then they will fast. The saying anticipates the passion without naming it.
16: epiblemma rhakous agnaptou — a patch of unprocessed cloth (agnaptos, not yet fulled or shrunk: raw, undressed fabric) — the patch is the new element that is problematic, not the garment. Airei gar to plērōma autou apo tou himatiou — for the patch takes away (airō) its fullness/filling from the garment: the new patch, shrinking in the wash, pulls against the old garment, making the tear worse. The parable argues that Jesus's ministry is not a patch to be applied to existing religious structures but something categorically new requiring new containers.
17: oinos neos eis askous palaiois — new wine into old wineskins (askoi, wineskins made of animal hide) — old wineskins are already stretched from previous fermentation; new wine still fermenting generates pressure that will burst them. Rēgnyntai hoi askoi kai ho oinos ekchynetai kai hoi askoi apollyntai — the skins burst and the wine spills out and the skins are destroyed (apollymi, the same word for perishing/losing): double loss. Oinos neos eis askous kainous — new wine into new wineskins: kainos (new in quality, fresh) vs. neos (new in time). The new wine of the kingdom requires the new wineskins of the new covenant community.
18: archōn eis proselthōn prosekynei autō — one ruler came and was bowing before him (prosekynei imperfect: was continuing to bow/worship in ongoing prostration) — archōn (ruler, official): Luke 8:41 identifies him as a synagogue ruler (archisyagōgos). Hē thygatēr mou arti eteleutēsen — my daughter has just died (arti, just now; teleutaō aorist: has come to her end). Alla elthōn epithes tēn cheira sou ep autēn kai zēsetai — but come and place your hand on her and she will live: the petition presupposes a power over death that goes beyond normal healing expectation.
19: egertheis ho Iēsous ēkolouthei autō — Jesus, having risen, was following him — egertheis aorist participle (having risen, i.e., from table or from his position): the same verb as the paralytic's rising in v.7. Ēkolouthei imperfect: was following, in process; notably Matthew here describes Jesus using the discipleship-verb (akoloutheo) in Jesus's own action of following the ruler — a remarkable reversal of the usual direction.
20: gynaik haimorrousa dōdeka etē — a woman hemorrhaging for twelve years — haimorrousa present active participle of haimorroō (to flow with blood): the present marks the continuous, unceasing nature of her condition. Twelve years of hemorrhage would have rendered her perpetually unclean under Lev 15:25-27, barring her from the Temple, from marriage, and from normal social contact. Proselthōn opisthen ēpsato tou kraspedou — approaching from behind she touched the hem (kraspedon, fringe/tassel of a garment, possibly the tassels of Num 15:38-39): approaching from behind conceals her unclean status.
21: elegen gar en heautē — for she was saying within herself — en heautē (within herself, in her own interior): the speech is internal, not spoken aloud. Ean monon hapsōmai tou himatiou autou sōthēsomai — if I only touch his garment I will be saved/healed (sōthēsomai future passive of sōzō): the MKT renders this "healed" but sōzō is Matthew's word for eschatological salvation as well as physical restoration. The woman's faith operates in the register of the word's full semantic range: she expects rescue.
22: epistrapheis kai idōn autēn eipen — turning and seeing her he said — epistrapheis aorist passive participle of strephō (to turn, be turned): a deliberate turning toward her. Tharsei thygate — take heart, daughter (thygate: the same affectionate address as teknon in v.2): a personal word before the pronouncement. Hē pistis sou sesōken se — your faith has saved/healed you — sesōken perfect active: it has saved and the saving stands completed. Kai esōthē hē gynē apo tēs hōras ekeinēs — and the woman was saved/healed from that hour: aorist passive marking the moment of completion.
23: tous aulētas kai ton ochlon thorybоumenon — the flute players and the noisy crowd (aulētēs, flute player; thorybeomai, to be in an uproar, to create a commotion) — in Jewish custom, even the poorest families hired professional mourners and musicians at a death; the aulētai would be playing dirge-music. The presence of formal mourning-professionals establishes that the girl is understood to be genuinely dead by those present.
24: anacōreite ou gar apethanen to korasion alla katheudei — withdraw, for the girl has not died but is sleeping (anacōreō, withdraw; katheudō, to sleep — also used as a euphemism for death in Jewish and Christian usage) — kategelōn autou (they laughed at him — katagelō, to laugh down at, deride): the crowd's laughter is not disrespect but certainty; they know she is dead. Jesus's statement is either metaphor (the death is temporary, like sleep) or a claim that from his perspective death has this quality. Both may be simultaneously true.
25: ekblēthentos de tou ochlou eiselthōn ekratēsen tēs cheiros autēs — when the crowd was put outside, entering he took her hand (ekballō aorist passive: the crowd was thrown out — the same verb as exorcism; krateō aorist: grasped firmly) — kai ēgerthē to korasion (and the girl arose — ēgerthē aorist passive of egeirō, the resurrection verb): the brevity of the narrative matches the immediacy of the event. No word of command is recorded in Matthew; the touch alone suffices.
26: kai exēlthen hē phēmē autē eis holēn tēn gēn ekeinēn — this report went out into all that land — phēmē (report, reputation, from phēmi, to say): the fame that Jesus repeatedly tries to limit (8:4; 9:30) spreads despite his restrictions. Holēn tēn gēn ekeinēn (all that land, the surrounding region): the news cannot be contained to the house or town.
27: huie Dauid eleēson hēmas — Son of David, have mercy on us — the blind men use the Davidic Messiah title (huios Dauid), which carries the expectation of the Messianic heir who would heal and restore Israel (Isa 29:18; 35:5). This is Matthew's first significant use of the title since the genealogy (1:1); it will reappear at 12:23; 15:22; 20:30-31; 21:9,15. Eleēson aorist imperative of eleō: grant mercy, show covenant loyalty.
28: pisteute hoti dynasthai touto poiēsai — do you believe that I am able to do this (pisteuo present: are you maintaining this belief?) — Jesus asks the question privately, indoors, after the public pursuit. Dynasthai (to be able): the question targets the object of their faith — not whether they will be healed, but whether Jesus specifically has the power. Nai kyrie — yes, Lord: kyrie (Lord) is their address, matching the centurion and the storm-disciples; the blind recognize what the crowds did not.
29: kata tēn pistin hymōn genēthētō hymin — according to your faith let it be done to you — kata (according to, in proportion to): the healing corresponds to the faith, not as a reward for it but as its completion. Genēthētō aorist passive imperative of ginomai: let it happen, let it come to be. The statement is remarkable: faith is the measure by which the miracle is sized. This principle recurs in 8:13 (the centurion: "as you believed") and 15:28 (the Canaanite woman: "as you desire").
30: enebrimēthē autois ho Iēsous — Jesus sternly warned them (embrimaomai: to snort with emotion, to charge sternly, to rebuke with intensity — the word is used for strong emotional reaction, including indignation; Mark uses it for Jesus's reaction before raising Lazarus, John 11:33) — horate mēdeis ginōsketō (see that no one knows: present imperative + subjunctive: an ongoing prohibition). The severity of the warning underlines the messianic secrecy motif: Jesus is managing the pace and nature of disclosure about his identity.
31: hoi de exelthontes diephēmisan auton en holē tē gē ekeinē — but going out they spread his fame throughout all that land — diaphēmizō (to spread a report widely, to publicize): the disobedience to the command of v.30 mirrors the spreading fame after the ruler's daughter (v.26). The inability to suppress news of miraculous healing is a structural theme in Matthew's miracle chain (ch8-9): the disciples leak the news despite the charge.
32: kōphon daimonizomenon — a mute/deaf demon-possessed man (kōphos: the word covers both deafness and muteness; context — the man not speaking — suggests muteness here) — the case is brought to Jesus by others, as in 8:16. This is the fourth major miracle in the sequence of ch9 (paralytic, hemorrhaging woman/Jairus's daughter, blind men, mute demoniac), each addressing a different incapacity that Second Temple Judaism associated with the messianic age.
33: ekblēthentos de tou daimoniou elalēsen ho kōphos — when the demon was driven out the mute man spoke — the exorcism and the cure are simultaneous: the demonic cause removed, the human capacity restored. Ethaumasan hoi ochloi legontes oudepote ephanthē houtōs en tō Israēl — the crowds marveled saying: never has it appeared thus in Israel (phainō aorist passive: never has it been seen; oudepote as in 7:23: never at any time). The cumulative effect of the miracle-chain provokes a climactic statement of unprecedented occurrence.
34: hoi de Pharisaioi elegon en tō archonti tōn daimoniōn ekballei ta daimonia — but the Pharisees were saying: by the ruler of demons he drives out demons — elegon imperfect: they kept saying, making this a sustained accusation. Ho archōn tōn daimoniōn (the ruler of the demons): the claim that exorcistic power comes from demonic source rather than divine; Jesus addresses this explicitly in 12:25-29. The crowd's amazement (v.33) and the Pharisees' accusation (v.34) divide the audience into two responses that will persist throughout the Gospel.
35: periēgen ho Iēsous tas poleis pasas kai tas kōmas — Jesus went around all the towns and villages (periagō imperfect: was continuously going about) — the three present participles (didaskōn, kērysson, therapeuōn: teaching, proclaiming, healing) describe the ongoing character of his itinerant ministry. Kēryssōn to euangelion tēs basileias — proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom: euangelion (good news, the announcement of victorious news by a herald) + tēs basileias (of the kingdom). The summary verse (cf. 4:23) uses the same triadic description of Jesus's ministry.
36: esplanchnisthē peri autōn — he had compassion on them (splagchnizomai, from splanchnon/intestines, the visceral seat of emotion; the strongest Greek word for compassion, meaning gut-level, visceral feeling for another's suffering) — eskylmenoi kai errimmenoi (harassed and helpless/cast down: skyllomai, to be harrassed, worn out; rhiptō perfect passive: thrown about, cast down like discarded things). Hōsei probata mē echonta poimena — like sheep not having a shepherd: echoing Num 27:17; 1 Kgs 22:17; Ezek 34:5 — the image of Israel without faithful shepherding.
37: ho men therismos polys hoi de ergatai oligoi — the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few — therismos (harvest) shifts the metaphor from sheep/shepherd (v.36) to harvest: the compassion-move produces a harvest-assessment. Polys/oligoi (plentiful/few): the asymmetry between need and available workers is the premise for the prayer of v.38. Harvest in Jewish eschatological imagery (Joel 3:13; Isa 27:12) carried end-time associations of gathering the nations.
38: deēthēte oun tou kyriou tou therismou — therefore pray/beseech the Lord of the harvest (deomai aorist passive imperative: to beseech, petition urgently) — tou kyriou tou therismou (the Lord of the harvest): God is the owner of the harvest field and the ultimate agent of mission; workers are sent by him, not self-commissioned. Hopōs ekbalē ergas eis ton therismon autou — that he might send out workers into his harvest (ekballō aorist subjunctive: to throw out, drive out, send out forcefully — the same verb for exorcism; mission is as urgent as exorcism). The prayer becomes the transition into the sending of the twelve in ch10.
